agent_approach_summary: The agent inspected MessageComposer, ReplyPreview, related profile components, and CSS, then started trying to recreate a much larger UI redesign from surrounding clues, including adding a new CancelButton component and CSS imports. It never made the minimal change in MessageComposer needed to render the tombstone notice semantically.
difficulty_evidence: The fail-to-pass test is straightforward and matches the problem statement: it still checks that tombstoned rooms do not render SendMessageComposer or MessageComposerButtons, and now additionally checks that a paragraph element contains text saying the room has been replaced. The agent got sidetracked implementing unrelated parts of the large gold patch and exited without submitting a fix.
reasoning: This task looks clean. The problem statement explicitly says the tombstone/replacement notice should use semantic HTML markup such as paragraph elements and clearly communicate that the room has been replaced. The F2P test checks exactly that by replacing the old class-based assertion with `wrapper.find("p").text()).toContain("room has been replaced")`. That is directly derivable from the spec and does not lock the solver to hidden internals beyond the stated semantic requirement. Although the gold patch is very large and includes many unrelated UI/layout changes, those are overpatch noise: the test only cares about the tombstoned-room composer behavior described in the prompt. In the trajectory, the agent did inspect the relevant MessageComposer code and could see the existing non-semantic div/span structure, so it understood the issue. However, it then chased a broader redesign, creating a CancelButton and CSS plumbing instead of applying the minimal semantic markup fix, and ultimately timed out without a submitted patch. That points to agent failure, not contamination.
